summary: This mock interview focuses on designing a file syncing system similar to Dropbox, iCloud, or Google Drive. The candidate, Alex, an engineering manager, clarifies requirements, performs back-of-the-napkin calculations, outlines a high-level architecture including client, load balancer, API servers, cloud storage (S3), and a metadata database (RDS/MySQL). He details API endpoints, client-side responsibilities (like compression), and a database schema. The discussion then moves to system refinements, including regional deployment, S3 key partitioning, CDN integration, client-side encryption, database replication, handling read/write traffic, and supporting folders, concluding with a note on conflict resolution.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So, what are we designing today, Alex? I wanted to ask you to design a system like Dropbox, iCloud, or Google Drive.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, that makes sense. I've used a lot of these apps. I think I was an early Dropbox sign-up in their early days. As I understand this app, it's generally a file syncing app. The apps do a lot of things and integrate with a lot of other systems, but to keep this within a 45-minute discussion, would it be fair to say that the main features we're looking at are downloading and uploading files, and then syncing it between devices?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, that's correct. But it also needs to have a way to notify clients that updates have occurred so they can receive the changes.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, interesting. Let me just write these requirements down so that I don't forget them: uploading, downloading, sync, and then notifications. And so, within these files, like specifically the files that are being uploaded, is there any restriction on the type of files that you can upload?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: No, all file types are supported. This system is blind to the specific file contents, so for example, videos and photos won't be previewed in the app to keep this design simple.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, that makes it a lot more simple. So, let's just say any file type, so we don't have to preview videos or anything like that. Is this going to be a desktop app, or are there any other clients?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, it will be a mobile app, a web app, and a desktop app. Let's say that the company wants this app to be the easiest and most trusted platform to sync your files.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, that is a good ethos to run with. I think that helps kind of guide a lot of the decisions as well. So, some nuts and bolts here: how many users does the product have?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, let's say 100 million users signed up, with one million daily active users.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: One million, okay, that's not too bad. So, how many files get uploaded each day per user?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I think we can assume that each user uploads one file a day on average. Some may be zero, but some will be zero, so let's average it out to one a day.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, what's the average file size?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Let's assume each file is about five megabytes average.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: That makes sense. Is there a limit per user?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, let's say each file must be 10 gigabytes or smaller, and then there's a 15 gigabyte limit per user account.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, okay. So, let me just look through here. So, the general features, the functional requirements that we're going to work on for this app are uploading, so that's going to be when you create or you upload content, that's going to trigger that uploading action of our app. Downloading files, which makes sense to a lot of us, but just to state the obvious, when other clients will do this when a create or an update has happened, so you upload something on your phone, your desktop will go and download that file. So, that'll create the feature that does a sync across devices, sort of the magic that pulls it all together. And then notifications, so that's going to happen when I upload a file on one device, it's going to sort of send that notification to make sure that everything is in sync. So, that sort of makes sense for the functional requirements.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think outside of the scope, we've got a few maybe sort of commonly thought of use cases for these apps. The number one I think of is previewing of files. Oftentimes, if it's a video, there'll be a play button so you can watch it, or if it's a photo, there'll be a thumbnail so you can sort of see what 'pictures.jpeg' actually is. We're not going to cover that to kind of keep this within scope. Also, I guess editing of content. So, in Google Drive, if you put in a Google Doc, you can actually edit that doc. That sort of functionality that they've added, we're not going to cover that. And then sharing of files as well. I think that's sort of outside of scope. I assume that sharing of files will be very similar to the downloading flow, so just to keep this discussion fast, we're not going to design it today, but just understand that a file could be shared outside to a non-logged-in user. We're just not going to write about that in the flowchart today. Does that make sense?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, yeah, that makes sense. I think that's the right way to limit the scope. And also, to keep this sort of within the requirements, it's not a backup app. It's not going to save old file versions, so it's just going to have the latest version. If you delete that file, there's not going to be a way to restore your backup or anything like that, just to keep us into the current sync. It's just kind of syncing the main files.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: There's also some non-functional requirements here. So, simplicity and trust. So, it's the easiest and most trusted platform to use. With that simplicity, it means to me it's available on any platform, so we've got all these different apps: a mobile app, a web app, a desktop. Also, high availability. If you need that resume at noon, you've got to be able to access and download it. And it also has to be fast and not a major bandwidth hog. And I think trust means that we cannot lose any data. So, even though maybe this is a free service, I know Dropbox has paid plans, but a lot of them start out as free. Even with free, we want consistent data and high availability of the service. I think that sort of makes a bit of sense here. Do you concur?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, yeah, I agree with all that. Sounds good.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Perfect. So, let's do some back-of-the-napkin math here. So, 100 million users signed up, and they can have 15 gigabytes per user. So, I'm just going to go into Google here and type in 1 million times 15 gigabytes. So, we're looking at 1.5 petabytes of data, 1500 terabytes. That's quite a bit of data, but not unreasonable. Let me say here, data total. And then we've got 1 million daily active users. So, let's divide this out. We've got a million divided by 24, divided by 3600 seconds in an hour. So, we're looking at about 11 queries per second, requests per second, QPS. Let's say 11. And at peak, we might be getting double that, so let's say peak QPS is like 20 per second. That's not terrible. And then what else do we have? Ah, we have traffic. So, average file is five megabytes, and we have a million daily active users. So, a million times five megabytes, I think we're looking at five terabytes data. Traffic five terabytes. Again, it's nothing groundbreaking here. I don't think we have to redesign the internet for this. Does this make sense? Is this good so far?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yep, this is good so far.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, perfect. When you come into this, you often might get stuck in this phase for too long, asking questions about the system, asking about numbers, asking specifics about users, traffic. You can ask a million questions about data points on an app, but in the end, I think there are only three ones that really matter here: one million daily active users, and how many they download a day, and how big the files are. So, being able to find, you might ask 10 pieces of data, but your end design is going to rely on maybe two to three of them, maybe not always, but don't spend too much time in this area. And also, rely on the interviewer. They are there as a sounding board. So, you might say, 'How many users do they have?' And they might say, '100 million.' They might also say, 'I don't know, figure it out, like how many do you think they would have?' And you have to be prepared with something logical at that point. And I think either way, the key is that even if they don't give you the answer, the key is to go to the interviewer and ask them, and always wrap up with that: 'Is there anything else I've left before I move on? Is this it?' And really give them a chance to guide you. So, I think I'll start jumping into the higher-level design here and starting with a higher-level concept. Sometimes you might start with a client and a single computer, but we've already identified that there is this need for trust, so we can't be losing, we can't be putting this on a USB stick and forgetting where we place it. It has to be sort of backed up from day one. So, I think I'm going to start with cloud storage in the initial design. Also, I'm sure that most of these services like Google Drive probably started its life in a data center; it probably didn't start in a closet at Google. So, it kind of makes sense to design this at least slightly robust from day one. So, let me move around here to a fresh spot. Okay, so the first component I think we're going to add into this is going to be a load balancer to handle all this traffic. So, we're probably getting a lot of traffic, and let's just say we've got the lovely orange, let's just say we're using AWS, the Elastic Load Balancer. It could really be anything, we could make custom servers at some point. This is really just a block that we're using in here, so don't really have to worry, not specific about how it works, but it's just a layer in between the application servers, which we're going to draw in next. Ah, perfect shape here for this. So, this is going to handle our application logic, and again, this isn't specific. This could be Amazon EC2 or Elastic Container Services, which allow you to use Kubernetes. So, however you've got your app set up, that's this block here. So, yeah, a request is going to come in, boop, it's going to go from there to get served to one of the application servers. What else do we have? Well, we've already identified we want cloud storage, so let's bring that in. Now, maybe I should talk about the APIs a little bit before we get too deep into how this all works. So, in this case, let's just say this is S3. Yeah, before I draw any connections of how the client interacts with here, I'm going to define a block with the APIs. Okay, so I'm going to jump down here. I'll try to keep it at that zoom level, and we're going to draw another section to talk about the APIs. So, we've got a few basic operations in the app here that we figured out at the beginning that we're going to need. So, I'll just draw them here, or write them out. We've got upload, we've got download, and we have get file revisions. Okay, so we've got these three components. So, uploading, this is going to be resumable uploads. So, S3 supports resumable uploads, so this is a feature we can use immediately from day one. And so, if you have a large video, I'm assuming we don't want them to have to start over if they close their laptop to move to their couch. We want it to pick up in the middle and continue uploading, and that's going to sort of add to the trust of this app. We don't want people to be disappointed with the way it works, the way it fills up their bandwidth cap. So, by using S3's feature for multi-part uploads, we can cover that feature immediately. So, the endpoint is going to look something like, just unbold this, so it's just a download endpoint like that. And you're going to request, so when you send data to this endpoint as a client, you're going to send the data that you want to upload, so the actual video, and then the response will be blank. It'll just be a JSON 200 OK. So, you send an upload, if it works, the client says, 'Okay, we've got the upload,' and they'll be able to manage where they are in the upload download process. Same for downloading, so nothing special, but instead of getting the actual file from the server, that's going to tie up our API servers with sending these long files across the internet, and we really don't want that when we're designing a system of this scale. So, rather than tie up our API server serving these files, what we're going to do is we're going to, the response is going to be a redirect. And so, instead of getting the actual data back, you're going to get the location where the data is, and the client will go and handle all that on their own. Okay, so and that will be, so you will send a request, and that will be the file ID. Oh, and I don't have the endpoint here. We go, let's do the endpoint. It's going to be something like, I've got these backwards here, I guess. So, you will send something to the download endpoint, the file ID, and you'll get back a redirect. Great. So, another feature of S3 is you can give a temporary URL that gives access to an object. So, you aren't putting their documents publicly on the internet; you're giving this one file temporary access to get it. And that's what the app will prepare. So, you want to request a file, you get a temporary way to get it. If you need it an hour later, you'll have to request again through our servers, and that sort of handles security and not tying up our servers with doing all the work here. Okay, so this endpoint, very similar, I can sense a pattern here. So, and we are going to request the file ID again, and you'll get back a list of changes along with a timestamp, and so that'll be enough to say, 'Is this file the latest version or not?' And do any sort of reconciliation of data if, you know, a client goes offline, goes on vacation for a month, and comes back, should be enough data with this. Let me just think here for a moment, see if I'm missing anything. Okay, does this make sense so far?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So, in the high-level design, you're outlining the main building blocks of the app, and when you start it, you're only going to have one or two of them. It's not going to be a complete picture. So, I think the important thing to keep in mind is keep building out an entire flow. So, keep something in your mind, the path that the human takes is what I always say. You have a client, they come into a load balancer, they hit an API server, then what happens? And use that flow to prompt you on what to build next. And I would go through logically from beginning to end on each of those flows. So, I started with upload, made that all make sense, because that's the first thing you do when you create the app as a developer. You're going to launch the app on your computer and you're going to upload a file in it to test it, and so it's very simple to think like, 'How would I be building this? What tests would I be doing in my own keyboard to test that this is working?' From there, I think you want to have a good rationalization for why to use each component. I mean, my example, I said that I chose S3 as the backend storage, and one of the justifications was that it's very commonly used. So, people have read blog posts about it. When they join your company, they know what S3 is, they've likely used it in the past position, and so this adds a lot of benefit to choosing that. But it also shows the interviewer you have reasons for picking these tools. It's not just something that you're copying off of a blog post and replaying it; it's something that you know about and you have reasons for using it. Yeah, this makes sense so far. Yeah, I think we're good to deep dive into the system itself if you're ready.

We're not going to handle any of that from in our design specifically, but add that here, it all happens locally. The responsibilities of the client are going to be interacting with S3, so actually downloading the file, uploading it, and sending credentials and all the security stuff. It's going to be responsible for compression, so making the file smaller, that's going to be the client, and then logging into the service. It's going to store the credentials, it's going to send the credentials with requests if required. All that, we're just going to kind of back that into the client.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Why do you want to do the compression on the client instead of on the API server or elsewhere?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: It's a great question. I suppose you could put it anywhere. Some logic might say you put it on the API server because you're paying for the CPU there, and you're not causing the client to bear the brunt of compression. However, when you do the compression on the client's side, then that file, when it goes through cloud storage, when it gets sent over the internet, it is already compressed. So, it's a trade-off: you want to balance being nice to the client and them using their CPU to compress the file, or do we want to waste their bandwidth? And I think in this situation, the CPU cost to compress a file is going to be minor compared to somebody using up all their bandwidth on their internet connection. That would, I think, be worse. Nobody's going to notice that their CPU ran for an extra half second, so we're going to put that in the client, and it also allows you to handle stuff differently. Maybe you don't want to do that on the phone because of battery life, or maybe you do want to do it more heavily on the phone because of battery life. This lets you put the compression right where the problem is.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER

We want to be able to get something consistently. So, something like a NoSQL database, it can perhaps do that, but by default, it doesn't give that guarantee with the CAP theorem, consistency, availability, or partition tolerance. You're going to have to choose to kind of prefer one or the other. So, we want the data to be very consistent, and so that's why I'm going to choose MySQL. The other reason I'm going to choose MySQL is it's a kind of a simpler problem, it's just there's a lot of people who are knowledgeable about it. turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Let's kind of give a few of the ideas I have about making the system a little bit better. There's some stuff that I haven't fully drawn here. So, one of them is regionality. So, this system might be considered to be in its own box. So, there is sort of like a major system here. This looks awful, but we assume that this whole system is a self-contained box and can be replicated in other regions. So, this might be the North American incarnation of our file sharing service. However, suddenly you get very popular in the Euro Zone, and you want a server that's based in Europe. You might replicate this entire component aside from the cloud storage and move it regionally, and that'll, the load balancer will then route traffic locally, it'll be much faster for people. Then you also get the benefit of, you know, the nitty-gritty of running a server. You might have to shut it down for maintenance. What happens when North America goes down? Well, you can now flip over to your European Zone, which will have an exact view perspective of what's to be delivered in North America, and then your clients might get a slightly slower response for that afternoon, but it won't go down, which will kind of enhance the trust of the service. Something we didn't talk about is how we store files in S3. So, the S3 is like a bucket, and we might have user one file, user two file. We may put everything at the root level of the directory, like the very top level, and that may seem sort of naively not a problem. However, many successful cloud services that rely on S3 have run into this problem. It's maybe not obvious, S3 is not magical, it is limited to a certain number of files, a certain number of sizes, but the guarantee, and if you read the docs carefully, is that Amazon will partition this in the background and make it more available. So, if you suddenly have a bunch of data in user one, let's say this is too big, what it'll do is it'll actually split up this user one folder into separate folders. I'm just kind of drawing slashes here to indicate that, and that by adding folders, it allows S3 to be fast and do all its magic. However, your application might get errors at that time while it's doing that. And so, to get ahead of that process, I would recommend in this design to write something like, like 'app' and then, just for example, just some random data ahead of the user. So, let me get rid of this junk so it all shows up on one line if I can. So, your actual user will be padded with all this data at the beginning, and that extra string at the beginning is enough to kind of buy you a little bit more time from S3 before it crashes. There's one more component here that we didn't draw, and I think most production systems will have this in. I think everyone uses Amazon S3 with a CDN. So, the way that that would actually work, to redraw this a little bit with the CDN concept in mind, is the client interacts with the CDN. If the CDN has it, it sends the data. If the CDN doesn't, it gets it from storage, and now the CDN has it for future users. And that's just another system to save costs and also make things faster, more regionally aware. So, again, that European, North America, if you didn't have two data centers, a CDN would be another way to achieve that speed for local users. Out of scope, and something we specifically didn't cover, but I'll mention it because we're using S3, was backups or versions. And so, I can see kind of it being really attractive to offer. Dropbox has a paid plan, so perhaps they would want to offer versioning, and I only bring it up because you can actually do this for free with S3. So, by picking Amazon as a provider for this, you can just click a switch and have backups available for customers. Obviously, we bear the brunt on storing all those files, we pay for that, but even if we wanted to provide like version from one month ago, this could be a way that we could just instantly provide this functionality sort of for free without a lot of the engineering cost. What other improvements might I think of here? We didn't talk about it, but we might also add to responsibilities encryption, and why would we do encryption and why would we do it on the client? Data breaches sometimes S3 gets broken into, or one of your laptops gets stolen, and so there is a real danger of somebody accessing that and seeing somebody's private information. Now, if you encrypt it on the client side, that risk is kind of eliminated. So, I think most services of this caliber, any company with a legal team, is probably going to implement this feature. Again, I didn't talk about it because it's client-side, we can always add more features into the client, but it doesn't really affect the system in its overall architecture. Finally, the one thing I didn't go into detail with was the database setup. So, this is one block here, but realistically, you're probably going to have at least three more. One is going to be a slave, which is just copying everything, and a few others will be read replicas, which will also be available to provide. And so, if this main server, if this main server goes down, I don't know how to draw this, just a big giant triangle, then the application server will be able to go to its backup and instantly serve customers. This is sort of, this comes out of the box with RDS often, like you have the ability to set that up, but I did not mention it in the design. So, I think that would be worth mentioning, and also if you had different regions, the thing I initially talked about, you will definitely need databases in those regions. So, yeah, multiple databases, I think another consideration or improvement for this system.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And do we consider read traffic to be the same level as write traffic, and how does this system handle that?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Oh, interesting. Yeah, that's a really, really good point. So, this system might actually have others that are many, so we've got a lot of others, and they're all actually contacting the system as well, and they need access. What we might do then is have a separate system for them to go to. So, just quickly re-architecting, writing to this database, however, when they need to read, they will read from another set of databases, and likewise, when clients need to read, because the clients, let's say you share the link on a forum, 100 clients are coming to download it, many more, you'll actually have a separate metadata service to handle that, and it'll have its own copy of metadata that won't interfere, and also you'll have the CDN to handle that traffic. So, we sort of covered high read with the CDN at the last minute, but not the database side of it. So, yeah, I think a second master-slave here for reading would be appropriate.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And yeah, one other question, does this design account for folders?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Oh, really good question. Let me think about this for a moment. Ah, that's a good point. So, let me jump over to our schema and add in a little piece of detail about that. So, path, here's what I was trying to be as quick as possible, and so the path would look something, let's just go down here, it'll be 'me/doc text.txt'. Now, what if I had a file, I guess you're saying called 'me.doc'? How do I know that that's a file or how do I know that that's a folder? I suppose obviously you could do what I just did there and have this backslash and interpret it, but let's just say let's add a field here, 'is_folder' and make that Boolean, so it can be a representation of a file, and when you click that file in the app, you get to a page that is empty because it is actually a folder. Interesting bit of nuance there that I didn't capture. Once you've got a system like this designed, and you're asked to provide some improvements, how could you handle scaling, how could you handle this and that? The concept of a data center comes to mind instantly, because you can simply wrap a box around what you've already drawn, or most of the components of what you've drawn, and just say that we will add this feature by duplicating it. We now have Europe and we have an Asian Data Center, and by saying that, it adds a little bit of meta knowledge. You understand that these components can be separated. So, for example, S3 is global, your CDN is global, however, your API servers, load balancer, are regional, your clients are also regional. And so, putting these boxes and saying that you can scale your system regionally is a really easy way to level up the design and improve it without having to complicate the actual flowchart that you've just designed. I think another thing to mention at this stage are things that you haven't covered. So, you may have in mind a specific technique that you haven't done or a specific feature that your app doesn't have. The example I used was backups or versions. We didn't want it, but it's conceivable that one day somebody might ask about it. You may want a premium level of your app, and so these are the sorts of questions that interviewers will throw at you. I know we didn't design it with backups, but how would we implement that now? And so, for you to jump in and show your knowledge of S3, ah, it has versioning, it supports that out of the box. It shows that you understand how the components work, how they can be modified, how you can refactor this design to actually work in production. One more thing that was not talked about, this is a file system, and sort of the nitty-gritty of working with these file systems is you may have conflicts. And let's say you and I are both on two different computers, the same user account, I guess, based on our simplistic design, and we both upload a very important document with the same name at the same time. We both spend an hour composing it, and it both hits the server. What do we do? Do we delete one of them or the other? I think the system, to ensure trust, needs to consider conflict resolution. Usually, these sorts of apps will just put like a duplicate file with a timestamp if it can't figure out what's going on. It's not going to delete the file, it's going to still sync those two versions to two computers, but conflict resolution is kind of important here because you don't want anything to drop on the floor, you want to handle all your customers' requests.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah, I think that brings us to the end of the interview, Alex. Thanks for a really, really good design there. How was the experience for you being in the hot seat?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: It was great. It was a little nerve-wracking at times. I think one of the big pieces of advice is just getting used to the keyboard shortcuts and getting used to manipulating everything, but no, it was a lot of fun and kind of interesting to see things from the other side of the camera.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, well, thanks so much for doing it, and yeah, we'd love to have you back on another time. So, yeah, thank you, and yeah, cheers. Thanks a lot. Cheers.

training_pairs.question: What are we designing today, Alex? I wanted to ask you to design a system like Dropbox, iCloud, or Google Drive.
training_pairs.answer: Okay, that makes sense. I've used a lot of these apps. I think I was an early Dropbox sign-up in their early days. As I understand this app, it's generally a file syncing app. The apps do a lot of things and integrate with a lot of other systems, but to keep this within a 45-minute discussion, would it be fair to say that the main features we're looking at are downloading and uploading files, and then syncing it between devices?
training_pairs.follow_up: Yes, that's correct. But it also needs to have a way to notify clients that updates have occurred so they can receive the changes.

training_pairs.question: Within these files, specifically the files that are being uploaded, is there any restriction on the type of files that you can upload?
training_pairs.answer: No, all file types are supported. This system is blind to the specific file contents, so for example, videos and photos won't be previewed in the app to keep this design simple.
training_pairs.follow_up: Is this going to be a desktop app, or are there any other clients?

training_pairs.question: Is this going to be a desktop app, or are there any other clients?
training_pairs.answer: Yes, it will be a mobile app, a web app, and a desktop app. Let's say that the company wants this app to be the easiest and most trusted platform to sync your files.
training_pairs.follow_up: How many users does the product have?

training_pairs.question: How many users does the product have?
training_pairs.answer: Let's say 100 million users signed up, with one million daily active users.
training_pairs.follow_up: How many files get uploaded each day per user?

training_pairs.question: How many files get uploaded each day per user?
training_pairs.answer: I think we can assume that each user uploads one file a day on average. Some may be zero, but some will be zero, so let's average it out to one a day.

training_pairs.question: What's the average file size?
training_pairs.answer: Let's assume each file is about five megabytes average.
training_pairs.follow_up: Is there a limit per user?

training_pairs.question: Why do you want to do the compression on the client instead of on the API server or elsewhere?
training_pairs.answer: It's a great question. I suppose you could put it anywhere. Some logic might say you put it on the API server because you're paying for the CPU there, and you're not causing the client to bear the brunt of compression. However, when you do the compression on the client's side, then that file, when it goes through cloud storage, when it gets sent over the internet, it is already compressed. So, it's a trade-off: you want to balance being nice to the client and them using their CPU to compress the file, or do we want to waste their bandwidth? And I think in this situation, the CPU cost to compress a file is going to be minor compared to somebody using up all their bandwidth on their internet connection. That would, I think, be worse. Nobody's going to notice that their CPU ran for an extra half second, so we're going to put that in the client, and it also allows you to handle stuff differently. Maybe you don't want to do that on the phone because of battery life, or maybe you do want to do it more heavily on the phone because of battery life. This lets you put the compression right where the problem is.

training_pairs.question: Does that sort of make sense for the design here of the system?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, that makes sense to me. Yeah, I think we're getting kind of starting to get near to the end of the interview, so maybe you want to kind of make some refinements to your design in the remaining time before we run out of time and I can't improve it further.

training_pairs.question: And do we consider read traffic to be the same level as write traffic, and how does this system handle that?
training_pairs.answer: Oh, interesting. Yeah, that's a really, really good point. So, this system might actually have others that are many, so we've got a lot of others, and they're all actually contacting the system as well, and they need access. What we might do then is have a separate system for them to go to. So, just quickly re-architecting, writing to this database, however, when they need to read, they will read from another set of databases, and likewise, when clients need to read, because the clients, let's say you share the link on a forum, 100 clients are coming to download it, many more, you'll actually have a separate metadata service to handle that, and it'll have its own copy of metadata that won't interfere, and also you'll have the CDN to handle that traffic. So, we sort of covered high read with the CDN at the last minute, but not the database side of it. So, yeah, I think a second master-slave here for reading would be appropriate.

training_pairs.question: And yeah, one other question, does this design account for folders?
training_pairs.answer: Oh, really good question. Let me think about this for a moment. Ah, that's a good point. So, let me jump over to our schema and add in a little piece of detail about that. So, path, here's what I was trying to be as quick as possible, and so the path would look something, let's just go down here, it'll be 'me/doc text.txt'. Now, what if I had a file, I guess you're saying called 'me.doc'? How do I know that that's a file or how do I know that that's a folder? Interesting bit of nuance there that I didn't capture. Once you've got a system like this designed, and you're asked to provide some improvements, how could you handle scaling, how could you handle this and that? The concept of a data center comes to mind instantly, because you can simply wrap a box around what you've already drawn, or most of the components of what you've drawn, and just say that we will add this feature by duplicating it. We now have Europe and we have an Asian Data Center, and by saying that, it adds a little bit of meta knowledge. You understand that these components can be separated. So, for example, S3 is global, your CDN is global, however, your API servers, load balancer, are regional, your clients are also regional. And so, putting these boxes and saying that you can scale your system regionally is a really easy way to level up the design and improve it without having to complicate the actual flowchart that you've just designed. I think another thing to mention at this stage are things that you haven't covered. So, you may have in mind a specific technique that you haven't done or a specific feature that your app doesn't have. The example I used was backups or versions. We didn't want it, but it's conceivable that one day somebody might ask about it. You may want a premium level of your app, and so these are the sorts of questions that interviewers will throw at you. I know we didn't design it with backups, but how would we implement that now? And so, for you to jump in and show your knowledge of S3, ah, it has versioning, it supports that out of the box. It shows that you understand how the components work, how they can be modified, how you can refactor this design to actually work in production. One more thing that was not talked about, this is a file system, and sort of the nitty-gritty of working with these file systems is you may have conflicts. And let's say you and I are both on two different computers, the same user account, I guess, based on our simplistic design, and we both upload a very important document with the same name at the same time. We both spend an hour composing it, and it both hits the server. What do we do? Do we delete one of them or the other? I think the system, to ensure trust, needs to consider conflict resolution. Usually, these sorts of apps will just put like a duplicate file with a timestamp if it can't figure out what's going on. It's not going to delete the file, it's going to still sync those two versions to two computers, but conflict resolution is kind of important here because you don't want anything to drop on the floor, you want to handle all your customers' requests.
